summary: Gruber responds to Jared Newman's Time Techland piece criticizing him and Jason Snell as 'Apple enthusiasts' who dismiss Windows 8. He systematically dismantles Newman's arguments by showing they misrepresent what he and Snell actually wrote — neither called the iPad 'perfect' or 'magical,' nor argued Microsoft should 'throw everything out.' Gruber reiterates that the iPad's advantage isn't just touch but an entirely different computing model with different rules for resource consumption, battery life, and responsiveness. He argues that combining legacy Windows and a tablet UI in one device is fundamentally impossible to do well — you can't have it both ways. The piece concludes with the sharp observation that Microsoft's Windows 8 strategy reveals they don't believe a post-PC era exists at all.
key_points: Newman's critique relies on hyperbolic misrepresentations of what Gruber and Snell actually argued about Windows 8
The iPad's advantage over traditional PCs extends far beyond touch — it encompasses resource management, battery life, responsiveness, and conceptual simplicity
Combining a full desktop OS with a tablet UI creates irreconcilable conflicts around background processes, resource consumption, and peripheral management
Microsoft's Windows 8 strategy is not a response to the post-PC era but a bet that the PC era will never end
Wanting a single device that does everything is understandable but practically impossible — 'people in hell want ice water'
money_quotes: And disdain — defined in the New Oxford American as 'the feeling that someone or something is unworthy of one's consideration or respect; contempt' — is utterly the wrong word. Snell and I both made clear that the actual new Windows 8 UI work Microsoft showed is both innovative and appealing. Disappointment is not disdain.
Today's iPad is lacking and limited, like the Mac of 1985. But also like the Mac of 1985, I believe it foreshadows the future of personal computing.
The iPad is not merely about touch. Touch is the tip of the iceberg.
The iPad is a far slower machine than a modern MacBook in terms of raw hardware performance, but it feels faster in many ways, because you never have to wait for it.
Newman is saying it'd be nice to have it both ways. I'm saying you can't have it both ways. He's definitely right — it would be nice. But I'm pretty sure I'm right, too — that it can't be done.
And people in hell want ice water.
What Microsoft revealed this week is that they do not believe there is a post-PC era. They're banking that the PC era will never end.
key_insight: Microsoft's attempt to merge tablet and desktop computing in Windows 8 reveals a fundamental refusal to accept that the post-PC era demands entirely new computing paradigms, not hybrid compromises.
